courses.title: Hazmat Awareness — Recertification
courses.summary: Recognize, isolate, and notify. Refresh your decision frame for hazardous-materials incidents.
courses.credibility: Built with NFPA 472 alignment and reviewed by 3 fire-service SMEs.

blocks.body: On arrival, your first 60 seconds are about *recognition*, not action. The placard, container shape, and bystander reports together build the picture.

blocks.prompt: You see a diamond placard with the number 1203 on a tanker. What does that suggest?

options.label: Gasoline (flammable liquid)
options.feedback: Correct — UN 1203 is gasoline. Your isolation distance defaults to 50m and grows with fire involvement.

options.label: Corrosive liquid
options.feedback: Not quite — corrosives use class 8 placards, not UN 1203.

options.label: Compressed oxygen
options.feedback: Oxygen is UN 1072 with a class 2.2 (oxidizer) placard.

blocks.title: Procedural overview
blocks.body: On arrival, isolation is a *living* distance — it expands the moment you see vapor, heat, or victim symptoms, and only contracts once monitoring confirms the air. Wind, slope, and bystander position all push the perimeter outward. Treat 50 meters as the *minimum*, not the answer.

items.title: Field note
items.body: Two-thirds of preventable exposures happen in the first 90 seconds because the perimeter wasn't moved when the wind shifted.

blocks.title: Escalation pathway
blocks.body: If the product remains unidentified after 5 minutes, default to Tier 2 isolation (>150m) and request hazmat-1. Don't downgrade until a second confirming observation matches your initial read.
A precise sitrep beats a long one. Lead with location, product, isolation, and resources requested.

courses.title: Critical Thinking — Foundations
courses.summary: Recognize claims, separate evidence from assertion, and structure a defensible argument.
courses.credibility: Reviewed by your faculty advisor; aligned to the campus rhetoric standard.

blocks.title: Procedural overview
blocks.body: Every argument has a *claim* (what you assert), *evidence* (what supports it), and a *warrant* (why the evidence proves the claim). Most weak arguments skip the warrant — they show data and hope the connection is obvious.

courses.title: Lockout / Tagout — Refresher
courses.summary: Six-step LOTO sequence, energy-isolation verification, and exception handling for shift transitions.
courses.credibility: OSHA 1910.147-aligned; reviewed by site EHS lead.

blocks.title: Notify, shut down, isolate, lock, dissipate, verify
Procedural overview
blocks.body: LOTO is six steps in order — *notify, shut down, isolate, lock, dissipate, verify*. Skipping verify is the failure mode behind most stored-energy injuries: the device is locked, but the system still has potential energy waiting to release.

courses.title: Sepsis Bundle — May Update
courses.summary: Recognize early sepsis, initiate the bundle within the hour, and document defensibly.
courses.credibility: Aligned to SSC 2024 guidance; reviewed by ED and ICU leads.

blocks.body: qSOFA isn't a diagnosis — it's a flag. Two of three (RR≥22, altered mentation, SBP≤100) raises the bar for re-eval, not for protocol auto-launch.

blocks.prompt: Which combination most strongly suggests qSOFA-positive?

options.label: RR 24, GCS 14, SBP 96
options.feedback: Right — that's three of three. Re-evaluate now and document the trigger.

options.label: HR 110, Temp 38.4, WBC 13
options.feedback: Those are SIRS-leaning vitals. qSOFA specifically uses RR, mentation, and SBP.

options.label: Lactate 2.1 alone
options.feedback: Lactate informs severity but isn't part of qSOFA.

blocks.title: Procedural overview
blocks.body: In high-stakes resuscitations, the first 60 minutes determine the trajectory. Lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, and vasopressors if MAP stays under 65 after fluids. Order matters less than *velocity* — but cultures should precede antibiotics whenever feasible.

items.title: Clinical note
items.body: Deviation from the hour-1 bundle correlates with a 14% increase in secondary complications during the stabilization phase.

blocks.title: Escalation pathways
blocks.body: When fluid resuscitation fails to reach hemodynamic targets within the three-minute window, a Tier 2 escalation is mandatory. This involves activating the specialty surgical team and reserving Intensive Care Unit (ICU) resources.
